figures.caption: Figure 1. Comparison of reconstructed flow fields generated by conventional and proposed methods for the reduced-order representation of the high-fidelity flow field. The rows correspond to the streamwise, vertical, and spanwise velocity components, while the columns compare the reference simulation results with reconstructions obtained using POD, MD-CNN-AE , and the proposed MD-CNN-AE + VAE method.
Figure 2. (a) Computational domain showing the inflow direction and overall geometry. (b) Training domain with uniformly refined grid spacing, highlighting the region used for model

Figure 3. (a) Vehicle body geometries generated using principal component analysis (PCA), where each model corresponds to a different principal component (PC1) coefficient. (b) Detailed view of shape variations at the rearmost region of the vehicle body, illustrating the deformation associated

Figure 4. Overview of the proposed neural network architecture, consisting of an encoder that extracts latent representations from the input data and a branched decoder structure that reconstructs multiple output fields from each latent vector element.

Figure 6. Comparison of loss convergence over training iterations for Exp. 1 (blue) and Exp. 2 (orange). While both experiments exhibit similar loss values in the early stage, their behaviors diverge after approximately 10² iterations.
Figure 7. Representative temporal evolution of latent vector components z1–z4

Figure 8. Snapshots of the fluctuating components of the decomposed flow field at PC1 = 0.0. The streamwise (u), spanwise (v), and vertical (w) velocity components are shown for Exp. 1 (top row) and Exp. 2 (bottom row). The schematics above indicate the coordinate system and velocity directions.

Figure 9. Snapshots of reconstructed flow fields from 32 latent variables at PC1 = 0.0. The streamwise (u), spanwise (v), vertical (w) velocity components, vorticity, and velocity divergence are shown for the reference field (top row), Exp. 1 (middle row), and Exp. 2 (bottom row). The schematics above indicate the coordinate system and velocity directions.

Figure 13. Comparison of phase space trajectories for different experimental setups. The phase space is defined by the turbulent kinetic energy (TKE, ∫ 𝑘; 𝑑𝑉), TKE production (∫ 𝑃; 𝑑𝑉), and TKE dissipation (∫ 𝐷; 𝑑𝑉). From left to right, the panels display a 3D perspective view followed by 2D projections onto the 𝑃-𝑘, 𝑃-𝐷, and 𝑘-𝐷 planes. Distinct clusters for Ref. (blue), Exp. 1 (orange), and Exp. 2 (green) illustrate the shifts in the system's energetic state under each condition.

Figure 14. Temporal autocorrelation coefficient 𝑅(∆𝑡) of velocity fluctuations for PC1=0.0. The plot compares the decay of temporal correlation over a time interval ∆𝑡 [s] for the Ref. (blue), Exp. 1 (orange), and Exp. 2 (green).
